nuscale_ch01_0086.title: 8.10 Operating Philosophy for Maintaining Occupational Radiation Exposures as Low as Is Reasonably Achievable
nuscale_ch01_0086.summary: Section 8.10 states that the operating philosophy for maintaining occupational radiation exposures as low as is reasonably achievable (ALARA) is considered a site-specific responsibility. The NuScale standard design does not directly address this guidance, as it is to be implemented by the COL applicant or licensee at each site.

nuscale_ch01_0852.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.1.B Review Criteria for Phase 1 (Analysis)
nuscale_ch01_0852.summary: This section summarizes the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report’s conformance with regulatory criteria regarding the crediting of manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale’s approach aligns with the updated review criteria for all phases of D3 analysis, superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment remain the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0853.title: SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0: Crediting Manual Operator Actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) AnalysesC.2.B Review Criteria for Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation)
nuscale_ch01_0853.summary: This section summarizes NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for crediting manual operator actions in Diversity and Defense-in-Depth (D3) analyses, as outlined in SRP Appendix 18-A, Rev 0. It confirms that NuScale meets the review criteria for all phases (analysis, preliminary validation, integrated system validation, and long-term integrity), superseding previous guidance, while noting that certain aspects of probabilistic risk assessment (PRA) are the responsibility of the COL applicant.

nuscale_ch01_0025.title: Control Room Habitability System
nuscale_ch01_0025.summary: The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive safety feature designed to protect plant operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases by supplying clean, compressed air to the main control room (MCR), even during power loss. The system maintains positive pressure in protected areas and has enough air storage to support the MCR for at least 72 hours after an incident.

nuscale_ch01_0807.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.1 No requirements - null.
nuscale_ch01_0807.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria related to anticipated transients without scram (ATWS) and thermal hydraulic stability. The NuScale design partially or fully meets requirements by relying on internal diversity within its module protection system (MPS) rather than a separate diverse scram system, and it conforms to GDC 12 for thermal hydraulic stability. Some requirements are not applicable due to the unique features of the NuScale design.

nuscale_ch01_0087.title: 8.11 Applications of Bioassay for Uranium
nuscale_ch01_0087.summary: Section 8.11 discusses the application of bioassay for uranium, noting that most related regulatory guidance is site-specific and falls under the responsibility of the Combined Operating License (COL) applicant or licensee. The NuScale standard design certification only partially addresses requirements related to ensuring occupational radiation exposures are as low as reasonably achievable, with design-specific aspects incorporated as applicable.

nuscale_ch01_0803.title: SRP 15.8, Rev 2: Anticipated Transients without ScramII.3.A.i Provide a diverse scram system
nuscale_ch01_0803.summary: The NuScale design partially conforms to SRP 15.8, Rev 2 requirements for Anticipated Transients Without Scram (ATWS) by relying on internal diversity within the Module Protection System (MPS) rather than a separate diverse scram system. This approach is considered to meet the intent of the ATWS rule, although some criteria are not applicable or only partially met due to the unique features of the NuScale design.

edges.description: Section A discusses NuScale's philosophy and programmatic activities for maintaining occupational radiation exposures ALARA, while Section B outlines review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses, both reflecting different aspects of NuScale's overall safety design philosophy by addressing personnel safety and human factors in risk mitigation.

Section A discusses NuScale's approach to maintaining occupational radiation exposures as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA), while Section B outlines review criteria for human factors engineering and operator actions in safety analyses, both contributing to the overall safety design philosophy by addressing different aspects of personnel safety and operational reliability.

Section A outlines programmatic controls for occupational radiation exposure, while Section B describes a passive safety system (ECCS) that, by design, limits radioactive releases during accidents, both contributing to NuScale's overall safety design philosophy of minimizing radiation exposure.

Section A outlines the Phase 1 (Analysis) review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in D3 analyses, while Section B provides the Phase 2 (Preliminary Validation) review criteria for the same context, with both sections sequentially addressing different stages of the regulatory evaluation process for NuScale's safety design philosophy.

Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design, which exemplifies the safety design philosophy referenced in Section A's discussion of defense-in-depth and diversity, thereby providing a concrete example of how NuScale's philosophy is implemented.

Section B describes the passive Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design, which exemplifies the safety design philosophy referenced in Section A's regulatory criteria for defense-in-depth and diversity, thereby supporting the analysis of NuScale's safety philosophy.

Section A discusses NuScale's safety design philosophy in the context of system design and regulatory conformance for thermal hydraulic stability and ATWS events, while Section B addresses operational philosophy for radiation exposure control, both reflecting different aspects of NuScale's overall safety approach but without direct informational or causal connection.

Section A discusses NuScale's approach to meeting the intent of regulatory requirements for diverse scram systems as part of its safety design philosophy, while Section B provides the review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, which are relevant to evaluating the adequacy of NuScale's safety design features.

Section A discusses NuScale's approach to diversity in the module protection system to address ATWS events, while Section B provides review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, connecting them through their focus on diverse safety measures and regulatory conformance for risk mitigation.

Section A describes specific bioassay and monitoring applications that are part of the broader operating philosophy outlined in Section B for maintaining occupational radiation exposures as low as reasonably achievable (ALARA).

Section A discusses site-specific programmatic activities for personnel monitoring and dose recording, while Section B addresses criteria for crediting manual operator actions in defense-in-depth analyses; both sections relate to NuScale's safety design philosophy by addressing different aspects of human factors and operational safety, but they do not directly reference or specify each other.

Section A discusses NuScale's approach to meeting the regulatory requirement for a diverse scram system as part of its safety design philosophy, while Section B addresses the review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, both relating to how NuScale demonstrates compliance with NRC expectations for safety system diversity.

Section A discusses NuScale's approach to meeting the diverse scram system requirement for ATWS events, while Section B addresses the review criteria for crediting manual operator actions in diversity and defense-in-depth analyses, both relating to how NuScale demonstrates compliance with regulatory expectations for safety system diversity.

Section A describes how the NuScale design partially conforms to the regulatory requirement in Section B for providing a diverse scram system to address ATWS events, specifically by relying on internal diversity within the module protection system.
